html:
Apator S.A. 8QM.F, APT.WA

Apator is not a clean “smart meter rollout starts tomorrow” story anymore. That old framing is too stale. The better thesis is that a small, ignored Polish industrial has already survived the messy part of the cycle, is now printing near-record revenue with better margins, and is being valued as if the recent contract wins are temporary hardware orders rather than the beginning of a multi-year grid digitalization capex cycle.

The company makes electricity meters, gas meters, water and heat meters, switchgear, grid automation and utility software. In 2025, revenue was PLN 1.202 billion, only 2% below 2024’s record level, while adjusted EBITDA rose 15% to PLN 163.3 million and adjusted net profit rose 15% to PLN 72.9 million. Net debt/adjusted EBITDA finished 2025 at about 0.5x. The stock, at roughly EUR 5.38 in Frankfurt and PLN 24.10 on the more liquid Warsaw listing, is valued around 5x adjusted EBITDA and about 9 to 11x earnings, depending on share-count convention. That is too low for a business with regulated demand, local manufacturing relevance, grid modernization exposure and a shareholder return record.

The market’s mistake is subtle. Apator’s 2025 revenue did not explode because Poland’s smart meter rollout is still lumpy, export gas sales fell, and some electricity meter deliveries shifted. That makes the screen look mature. Underneath, the order base is improving. Enea’s smart meter partnership expanded to roughly PLN 159 million under the first call, with first batches delivered in January 2026. Energa-Operator awarded Apator a PLN 126.9 million remote-reading meter contract over 36 months, plus a separate PLN 28.8 million prepayment meter contract. Tauron signed a PLN 45 million network asset management IT contract for 2025 to 2028. PSG’s eGazomierz project gives Apator Metrix PLN 134.4 million of smart gas meter contracts through September 2026, tied to one of Europe’s largest gas meter replacement projects. In water, Powogaz won a EUR 4.7 million ultrasonic meter contract in Latvia and grew Water and Heat revenue 9% in 2025, with segment EBITDA up 30%.

This is why the stock is more interesting than the headline multiple. The company is moving from “meter hardware supplier” toward a hardware, communications, software, installation and maintenance stack. It is not a SaaS company, and pretending it is would be lazy. But the mix is becoming more valuable: ICT and automation recovered sharply in the second half of 2025, water and heat is scaling ultrasonic meters and remote reading, and grid operators need Polish and EU supply chains that meet cybersecurity and resilience requirements. Apator’s strategy explicitly targets smart meters, ultrasonic water meters, systems and services as higher-margin growth lines.

The regulatory backdrop is unusually favorable. Poland still has a large replacement runway, with the national smart meter target at 80% by 2028. Apator’s own strategy materials said only about 3.2 million smart meters had been installed at that point, around 15% of total replacements, and its goal is to maintain roughly 50% market share. DSOs are also receiving long-term low-interest financing for grid modernization through 2040. That matters because the buyer universe is concentrated, political and slow, but once tenders are awarded, the volumes are sticky and switching risk is real.

The downside is not imaginary. UOKiK opened antitrust proceedings against Apator Metrix related to historical gas meter tenders, creating a legal overhang. A theoretical maximum fine based on group revenue would be painful, though a penalty based more narrowly on the gas segment would be much less material. There was also a PLN 20.8 million warranty provision in Q4 2025 tied to a specific foreign-market electricity device. Management says the matter is settled, insured and not applicable to Polish deliveries, but it is a reminder that six-year warranties and utility contracts carry real execution risk. Export markets are also mixed, especially gas in Western Europe, and German industrial weakness has hurt some switchgear expectations.

The retail context is useful because it shows there is no hype premium. Polish forums and individual-investor coverage discuss Apator as a 2026 idea, but this is not a crowded global small-cap, not a meme stock, and not a fashionable AI or defense trade. The shareholder base is still mostly local value and dividend investors. That makes the rerating path simpler: Apator does not need a heroic narrative, it needs two or three quarters confirming that 2025’s margin improvement was not a one-off and that 2026 deliveries convert into cash.

My base case is FY2026 to FY2027 revenue of PLN 1.30 to 1.40 billion and adjusted EBITDA of PLN 180 to 190 million, with net debt staying below 1x EBITDA despite capex. At 7x EBITDA, still modest for a European grid equipment and utility digitalization supplier, equity value lands near PLN 35 per share, roughly EUR 8.25. The bull case is not multiple fantasy: if electricity meters, gas meters, water/heat ultrasonic products and ICT all hit together, Apator can reach PLN 210 to 225 million EBITDA. At 8x EBITDA, that supports roughly PLN 50 per share, or EUR 11.75.

Current stock price: EUR 5.38 on 8QM.F, with the primary Warsaw listing at PLN 24.10.

Bear target: EUR 4.20, about 22% downside. This assumes DSO delays, UOKiK or warranty costs, weaker exports and EBITDA stuck near PLN 135 to 145 million.

Base target: EUR 8.25, about 53% upside. This assumes contracted deliveries normalize, Water and Heat remains strong, and EBITDA reaches PLN 180 to 190 million.

Bull target: EUR 11.75, about 118% upside. This assumes Poland’s rollout accelerates, Apator holds high domestic share, ICT and automation scale, and EBITDA reaches PLN 210 to 225 million.
